stages: Brainstorm different family structures students know from their lives and media.
Discuss what legal rights and protections might mean in family contexts.
Sort family structures by their legal status and protections.
Create descriptors for what understanding looks like at different levels.
student_prompts: What different types of family relationships do you see around you?
What legal rights or protections might families need?
How might legal protections differ between married couples and those living together?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose examples that clearly show different levels of understanding legal distinctions between marriage, civil partnership, and cohabitation.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to identify legal rights for family relationships.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can name some family structures but I mix up their legal protections.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can identify basic family structures but I miss some legal distinctions.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can accurately identify legal rights and protections for marriage, civil partnership, and cohabitation.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can compare legal protections across family structures and explain implications for different situations.

self_check_prompts: Can I explain the legal differences between marriage, civil partnership, and cohabitation?
Do I understand what rights and protections each family structure provides?

no_evidence: Provide a simple list of family structures to start with.
Ask students to share one family type they know.
emerging: Help clarify which legal protections belong to which family structure.
Use comparison charts to organize legal rights by family type.
developing: Point out specific legal distinctions the student missed.
Provide focused practice on areas of confusion.
competent: Challenge students to consider how these legal differences affect real situations.
Ask them to explain implications for inheritance, medical decisions, or property rights.

stages: Brainstorm what makes families different from each other and how they might change

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to explain how families change or what makes parenting effective

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some family changes or parenting features with help but my explanations are incomplete

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can explain basic family changes and describe some parenting characteristics but without much depth

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can explain how families change over time and describe characteristics of effective parenting and child development on my own

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can connect family dynamics to broader social factors and analyze complex relationships between parenting and development

self_check_prompts: Can I explain specific ways families change and why effective parenting matters?
Am I making connections between family dynamics and bigger social influences?

no_evidence: Provide specific examples of family changes to get started
Ask what they notice about families they know
emerging: Guide them to complete their explanations with follow-up questions
Provide sentence starters for describing parenting characteristics
developing: Push for deeper explanations by asking why these changes happen
Encourage connections between parenting styles and child outcomes
competent: Challenge them to consider how society influences family dynamics
Ask them to analyze complex parenting scenarios with multiple factors

stages: Brainstorm what equality means in relationships and why it matters

levels.name: emerging

levels.name: developing

levels.name: competent

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can apply equality and self-worth principles to analyse complex relationship scenarios and broader social contexts

self_check_prompts: Can I clearly explain what equality, self-esteem, and respectful treatment mean?
Can I show how these three concepts connect to create healthy relationships?

emerging: Help connect identified aspects of equality to specific relationship examples
Provide sentence starters for explaining concepts more completely
developing: Guide exploration of how self-worth influences respectful behavior
Use scenarios to practice connecting all three concepts together
competent: Present complex relationship scenarios for deeper analysis
Encourage application of principles to broader social justice contexts

stages: Students share experiences or observations of different ways people can be mean to others
Class categorizes these behaviors into groups and names the different types
Students discuss how each type might make someone feel
Class refines categories and impact descriptions together
Students test the rubric by applying it to scenarios

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet identified types of bullying or explained their impact on victims.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can name some types of bullying with help but my explanations are incomplete.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can identify several types of bullying and explain basic impacts but miss some important details.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can identify different types of bullying and explain how each impacts victims accurately and completely.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can analyze complex bullying situations and explain detailed long-term impacts on victims.

self_check_prompts: Can I name at least three different types of bullying?
Can I explain how each type affects the person being bullied?
Are my explanations detailed and accurate?

no_evidence: Provide examples of bullying situations and ask what type each one represents
Guide student to notice how victims might feel in simple scenarios
emerging: Ask student to elaborate on incomplete explanations with specific details
Provide sentence starters to help structure more complete responses
developing: Point out missed distinctions between bullying types and ask for clarification
Prompt student to consider additional consequences they may have overlooked
competent: Challenge student to analyze more complex scenarios with multiple bullying types
Ask student to consider long-term psychological and social impacts beyond immediate effects

stages: Students share experiences about times they needed help with problems.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet identified any sources for getting help with bullying.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can name some people who might help with bullying but need support understanding which ones are best.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can identify help sources for bullying but need help choosing the most appropriate methods.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can independently determine appropriate sources and methods for getting help in different bullying situations.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can evaluate multiple help-seeking strategies and adapt my approach based on specific bullying contexts.

self_check_prompts: Can I explain why this help source is appropriate for this specific bullying situation?
Have I considered multiple ways to get help and chosen the most effective method?

no_evidence: Guide student to identify one trusted adult they could talk to about problems.
Provide examples of help sources like teachers, counselors, or family members.
emerging: Help student think about what makes a help source trustworthy and safe.
Practice matching simple bullying scenarios with appropriate help sources.
developing: Support student in comparing different methods of seeking help for the same situation.
Guide reflection on why timing and approach matter when seeking help.
competent: Challenge student to consider how context changes the best help-seeking approach.
Encourage evaluation of backup plans when first help source is unavailable.

stages: Students share examples of stereotypes they've encountered or heard about.
Class discusses what makes these examples harmful to individuals and groups.
Students identify patterns in how stereotypes lead to unfair treatment.
Class develops criteria for explaining stereotype harm clearly and accurately.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to explain how stereotypes cause harm.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some stereotypes with help but my explanations of harm need work.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can identify stereotypes about protected characteristics but need to explain the damage more clearly.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can explain how stereotypes about protected characteristics cause damage and encourage prejudice.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can analyze how different stereotypes work together and affect whole communities.

self_check_prompts: Have I clearly explained both the damage stereotypes cause and how they encourage prejudice?
Did I focus on stereotypes about protected characteristics specifically?

no_evidence: Provide examples of common stereotypes to help students recognize them.
Ask students to think about times they've seen people treated unfairly.
emerging: Guide students to connect specific stereotypes to protected characteristics.
Help students think through the emotional and social impact of stereotypes.
developing: Encourage students to explain the connection between stereotypes and prejudicial behavior.
Ask students to be more specific about types of damage caused.
competent: Challenge students to consider how multiple stereotypes interact.
Prompt analysis of broader community and systemic effects.

stages: Brainstorm what power means in relationships and list different types of power people might have
Discuss how having more or less power might change how people behave toward each other

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to analyse power dynamics in relationships

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some power differences with support but my analysis needs more accuracy

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can independently identify power inequalities in relationships but need to deepen my analysis

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can independently analyse how power inequalities affect behaviour and respect with clear reasoning

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can transfer my analysis to complex scenarios and connect power dynamics across multiple relationship contexts

self_check_prompts: Can I explain how the power difference is actually changing how people behave?
Have I shown clear connections between power and respect in this relationship?

no_evidence: Start by identifying who has more power in this relationship scenario
Look for one clear example of different treatment between people
emerging: Focus on one specific power difference and explain exactly how it changes behaviour
Check your reasoning by asking whether your analysis makes logical sense
developing: Dig deeper by explaining why the power difference leads to these specific behaviours
Connect your observations to how respect is affected in this relationship
competent: Apply your analysis skills to a more complex relationship scenario
Look for patterns in how power dynamics work across different types of relationships

stages: Students brainstorm behavioural expectations they know from school and home.
Students discuss what good behaviour looks like online versus offline.
Students identify similarities between online and offline behavioural expectations.
Students create statements about how expectations stay consistent across contexts.
Students refine language to describe consistency of behavioural standards.
student_prompts: What are some behavioural expectations you follow at school or home?
How might these same expectations apply when you are online?
What makes good behaviour the same whether you are face-to-face or online?

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet addressed behavioural expectations across different contexts.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some behavioural expectations with help but get confused about online and offline standards.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can identify behavioural expectations on my own but struggle to explain how they stay the same across different contexts.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can explain how behavioural expectations remain the same across online and offline contexts.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can apply consistent behavioural standards to new situations and explain the ethical principles behind them.

self_check_prompts: Can I clearly explain how behavioural expectations stay the same whether I am online or offline?
Have I shown understanding that good behaviour doesn't change based on the context I am in?

Guide students to notice behavioural rules they already follow.
emerging: Help students separate what stays the same from what might be different online versus offline.
Provide clear examples of consistent expectations across contexts.
developing: Ask students to explain why behavioural expectations might remain consistent.
Prompt students to make connections between online and offline behavioural standards.
competent: Challenge students to apply their understanding to unfamiliar situations.
Encourage students to explore the ethical reasoning behind consistent behavioural standards.

stages: Students brainstorm what could go wrong when sharing images online

emerging: Guide student to be more specific about consequences
Prompt student to think about legal aspects beyond basic risks

Ask about consequences for sharing indecent images

Ask how different legal frameworks might apply together

stages: Students share experiences with concerning digital content they've encountered

levels.name: developing

levels.name: extending

levels.name: no_evidence

stages: Students share what they think they know about weapon carrying among young people.
Teacher presents actual data and statistics about weapon carrying prevalence.
Students compare their initial beliefs with the real data to identify gaps.
Class discusses where misconceptions come from and why they persist.
Students and teacher build criteria for demonstrating clear understanding versus misconceptions.
student_prompts: What do you believe about how common weapon carrying is among people your age?
How do these real statistics compare to what you expected?
What misconceptions might lead someone to think they need a weapon for protection?
How can we tell the difference between accurate information and misconceptions about weapon carrying?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose student responses that clearly show the progression from basic awareness to sophisticated analysis of how misconceptions develop in different communities.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet addressed weapon carrying facts or misconceptions.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some basic facts about weapons with help but still hold significant misconceptions.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can state some accurate information about weapon carrying prevalence but struggle to clearly identify key misconceptions.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can explain the actual prevalence of weapon carrying among young people and identify specific misconceptions about protection needs.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can analyze how misconceptions develop and persist in different community contexts beyond basic understanding.

self_check_prompts: Can I explain what the real data shows about weapon carrying among young people?
Can I identify specific misconceptions that lead to false beliefs about needing weapons for protection?

no_evidence: Provide basic statistics about weapon carrying prevalence to establish foundation.
Ask student to share one thing they think they know about weapon carrying.
emerging: Help student distinguish between accurate facts and personal beliefs or assumptions.
Provide specific data to correct the most significant misconceptions.
developing: Guide student to identify common misconceptions about protection needs.
Ask student to explain why their stated facts might differ from common beliefs.
competent: Challenge student to consider how these misconceptions develop in different communities.
Ask student to analyze factors that make misconceptions persist despite available data.

stages: Discuss what exploitation and grooming mean and why getting help matters
Brainstorm different types of people and places that could provide help
Explore what taking action looks like in different situations
Identify what makes help-seeking effective versus ineffective
student_prompts: What would you do if you or someone you know was in an unsafe situation?
Who are the trusted adults in your life that you could talk to?
What might stop someone from seeking help and how could we overcome those barriers?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose scenarios that show clear differences between hesitant versus confident help-seeking and between appropriate versus inappropriate sources of support.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet shown that I can identify help sources for exploitation concerns.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can name some help sources with support but feel confused about what actions to take.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can identify basic help sources for exploitation but find it hard to take effective action on my own.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can independently identify appropriate sources of help and take effective action when concerns about grooming or exploitation arise.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can demonstrate sophisticated help-seeking strategies and support others in addressing exploitation concerns effectively.

self_check_prompts: Can I name specific people or organizations I would contact if I had concerns about exploitation?
Do I feel confident about what steps to take if I or someone I know needed help with this issue?

no_evidence: Provide clear examples of trusted adults and organizations that help with safety concerns
Practice identifying warning signs of exploitation through guided discussion
emerging: Help clarify the difference between appropriate and inappropriate help sources
Role-play simple help-seeking scenarios with clear steps
developing: Practice taking action steps through structured scenarios
Build confidence by discussing what effective help-seeking looks like
competent: Explore more complex situations that require sophisticated help-seeking strategies
Discuss how to support others who might need help with exploitation concerns
